title: Movement and Positioning
description: Rules for movement, positioning, and spatial relationships in combat

basic_movement.description: On your turn, you can move a distance equal to your Speed or less. Or you can decide not to move
basic_movement.movement_types: Your movement can include climbing, crawling, jumping, and swimming (each explained in the Rules Glossary). These different modes of movement can be combined with your regular movement, or they can constitute your entire move
basic_movement.speed_deduction: However you're moving with your Speed, you deduct the distance of each part of your move from it until it is used up or until you are done moving, whichever comes first
basic_movement.speed_determination: A character's Speed is determined during character creation. A monster's Speed is noted in the monster's stat block. See the Rules Glossary for more about Speed as well as about special speeds, such as a Climb Speed, Fly Speed, or Swim Speed

difficult_terrain.description: Combatants are often slowed down by Difficult Terrain. Low furniture, rubble, undergrowth, steep stairs, snow, and shallow bogs are examples of Difficult Terrain
difficult_terrain.cost: Every foot of movement in Difficult Terrain costs 1 extra foot, even if multiple things in a space count as Difficult Terrain

grid_rules.description: If you play using a square grid and miniatures or other tokens, follow these rules

speed_conversion.method: Rather than moving foot by foot, move square by square on the grid, using your Speed in 5-foot segments
speed_conversion.calculation: You can translate your Speed into squares by dividing it by 5. For example, a Speed of 30 feet translates into 6 squares
speed_conversion.tip: If you use a grid often, consider writing your Speed in squares on your character sheet

entering_squares.cost: To enter a square, you must have enough movement left to pay for entering. It costs 1 square of movement to enter an unoccupied square that's adjacent to your space (orthogonally or diagonally adjacent)
entering_squares.difficult_terrain: A square of Difficult Terrain costs 2 squares to enter
entering_squares.other_effects: Other effects might make a square cost even more

grid_rules.corners: Diagonal movement can't cross the corner of a wall, a large tree, or another terrain feature that fills its space
grid_rules.ranges: To determine the range on a grid between two things—whether creatures or objects—count squares from a square adjacent to one of them and stop counting in the space of the other one. Count by the shortest route

breaking_up_movement.description: You can break up your move, using some of its movement before and after any action, Bonus Action, or Reaction you take on the same turn
breaking_up_movement.example: For example, if you have a Speed of 30 feet, you could go 10 feet, take an action, and then go 20 feet

dropping_prone.description: On your turn, you can give yourself the Prone condition without using an action or any of your Speed, but you can't do so if your Speed is 0

creature_size.description: A creature belongs to a size category, which determines the width of the square space the creature occupies on a map
creature_size.space_definition: That table lists the sizes from smallest (Tiny) to largest (Gargantuan). A creature's space is the area that it effectively controls in combat and the area it needs to fight effectively
creature_size.determination: A character's size is determined by species, and a monster's size is specified in the monster's stat block

moving_around_creatures.passable_creatures: During your move, you can pass through the space of an ally, a creature that has the Incapacitated condition, a Tiny creature, or a creature that is two sizes larger or smaller than you
moving_around_creatures.difficult_terrain_rule: Another creature's space is Difficult Terrain for you unless that creature is Tiny or your ally
moving_around_creatures.ending_movement: You can't willingly end a move in a space occupied by another creature
moving_around_creatures.unwilling_occupation: If you somehow end a turn in a space with another creature, you have the Prone condition unless you are Tiny or are of a larger size than the other creature

walking.cost: 1 foot of movement per foot traveled

difficult_terrain.cost: 2 feet of movement per foot traveled

climbing.description: Moving up, down, or across vertical surfaces
climbing.reference: See Rules Glossary for detailed rules

crawling.reference: See Rules Glossary for detailed rules

jumping.description: Leaping across gaps or over obstacles
jumping.reference: See Rules Glossary for detailed rules

swimming.reference: See Rules Glossary for detailed rules

positioning_tactics.flanking: Positioning to gain tactical advantage over enemies
positioning_tactics.cover_seeking: Using terrain and objects to gain protection
positioning_tactics.choke_points: Controlling narrow passages to limit enemy movement
positioning_tactics.formation_fighting: Coordinating positions with allies for maximum effectiveness
